title.emphasis: Part IV: Emerging Frontiers: Digital Assets and IP Securities

section.para: The tokenization of IP and the rise of decentralized governance structures introduce new complexities into the contracting landscape.

title.emphasis: A. Tokenization of IP (NFTs as Contracts)

section.para: Non-Fungible Tokens (NFTs) represent a new mechanism for contracting digital assets. An NFT is typically a verifiable record of ownership or a license tied to a digital file, rather than the IP itself. The legal relationship is governed by the "smart contract" embedded in the NFT and the associated terms of sale. The classification of NFTs—whether as goods, securities, or general intangibles—remains a subject of legal debate.

title.emphasis: B. Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs) as IP Holders

section.para: DAOs offer a novel structure for collective ownership and management of IP. Governed by code and consensus rather than a central authority, DAOs can pool resources to fund creation and manage licensing. However, their lack of formal legal status in many jurisdictions creates uncertainty regarding liability, contract enforcement, and the application of labor laws to contributors compensated in the DAO's native tokens.

title.emphasis: C. IP Securities and Fractional Ownership

section.para: The securitization of IP involves transforming future revenue streams from intellectual property (e.g., music royalties) into tradable financial assets. Fractional ownership models, enabled by blockchain technology, allow multiple investors to hold shares in a single piece of IP. These instruments are subject to securities regulations, requiring compliance with disclosure and registration requirements. The contracting challenge lies in accurately valuing the underlying IP and ensuring the clear transfer of rights to the investment vehicle.
